era_context: The year 763 CE falls in the immediate aftermath of the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE) in China, the consolidation phase of the Abbasid Caliphate in the Islamic world, and the period of Frankish expansion in Western Europe. This was a transitional year marked by the end of major conflicts and the beginning of new administrative phases in multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish realm

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion ends; Tang Dynasty enters recovery phase
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which had begun in 755 CE, was finally suppressed in 763 CE with the death of the rebel leader Shi Siming and the recapture of Chang'an and Luoyang by Tang forces. This eight-year conflict had devastated northern China, killed an estimated 10–15 million people (though this figure is debated), and fundamentally weakened the Tang Dynasty's military and fiscal capacity. The rebellion marked the end of the Tang's early imperial expansion and the beginning of a long period of decline, though cultural production continued. The recovery phase involved reorganizing the military, restoring administrative control, and dealing with the rise of regional military governors (jiedushi).

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu), compiled by Ouyang Xiu et al., 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu), compiled by Liu Xu et al., 10th century

events.certainty_note: The end of the An Lushan Rebellion in 763 CE is clearly documented in multiple Chinese historical sources. The date is well-attested, though casualty figures are debated by modern scholars.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate: al-Mansur consolidates power; suppression of Umayyad remnants
events.description: By 763 CE, Caliph al-Mansur had largely consolidated Abbasid power, though pockets of Umayyad resistance and other factional opposition remained. The year 763 saw continued efforts to suppress remaining Umayyad loyalists and establish centralized control over the vast Islamic empire. The Barmakid family, of Persian origin, was rising in prominence as administrators, signaling the shift toward a multi-ethnic, bureaucratic state. This consolidation was not without violence and purges, as al-Mansur eliminated potential rivals and established his authority.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of al-Mansur's consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 763 CE are not always precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Frankish Kingdom: Pepin III's final campaigns; preparation for succession
events.region: Frankish Kingdom (modern France, Germany, Low Countries)
events.description: Pepin III (r. 751–768 CE) continued his military campaigns in Aquitaine and the Pyrenees in the early 760s. By 763, Pepin was aging and preparing for the succession of his sons, Charlemagne and Carloman. The period 763–768 saw continued consolidation of Frankish power and military campaigns that would provide the foundation for Charlemagne's later expansion. Pepin's reign established the Carolingian dynasty as the dominant military power in Western Europe.
events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals provide near-contemporary documentation of Frankish military campaigns in the 760s, though specific events in 763 CE are not always detailed.

events.title: Sack of Chang'an by rebel forces during An Lushan Rebellion
events.description: During the final phase of the An Lushan Rebellion, Chang'an, the Tang capital, was sacked and occupied by rebel forces. The city was eventually retaken by Tang loyalist forces under General Guo Ziyi, but the destruction was immense. The sack of Chang'an symbolized the nadir of Tang power and the scale of the rebellion's devastation. The recovery of the capital was a crucial symbolic and strategic victory for the Tang, marking the beginning of the rebellion's end.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)

events.certainty_note: The sack of Chang'an during the An Lushan Rebellion is well-documented in Chinese historical sources. The date is clearly attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures for the An Lushan Rebellion (often cited as 10–15 million deaths) are debated by modern scholars. Some argue these figures are exaggerated in later sources, while others suggest they may be underestimated. Additionally, the narrative of the rebellion as a simple 'military revolt' obscures its complex origins in regional military autonomy, ethnic tensions, and fiscal crisis. The 'recovery' of the Tang after 763 CE was slower and more incomplete than traditional historiography suggests; the dynasty never fully regained its earlier military dominance.
historiographic_note: Sources for 763 CE are heavily concentrated in Chinese and Islamic historiography. Chinese sources are relatively detailed for the An Lushan Rebellion, though they reflect court perspectives and may underrepresent popular experiences. Islamic sources are less detailed for specific events in 763 CE and are often compiled centuries after the fact. Western European sources are sparse and often legendary. The year 763 CE is significant primarily for events in East Asia and the Islamic world; most of the rest of the world has no documented events for this year.

graph_edges.note: End of the An Lushan Rebellion that began in 755 CE
Sack of Chang'an was part of the final phase of the An Lushan Rebellion
Abbasid consolidation followed the revolution of 750 CE
Pepin III's campaigns prepared the way for Charlemagne's succession and expansion
